chunk_summary: This section discusses vegetarian planning for selective eating and feeding disorders, emphasizing structured dietary interventions, ARFID criteria, and multidisciplinary management strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the concept of 'few-food vegetarianism' in selective eating and its clinical implications.
Learn structured dietary planning for children with selective eating patterns, including protein anchors and exposure goals.
Identify ARFID diagnostic criteria and escalation triggers in feeding disorders.
Explore multidisciplinary approaches to managing feeding disorders in vegetarian contexts.
key_facts: Few-food vegetarianism often results in starch-heavy, protein-poor diets, distinct from planned lacto-vegetarian diets.
First-visit dietary plan includes a '3-anchor' structure: one protein anchor per meal, one energy-dense snack, and a non-negotiable breakfast.
ARFID criteria include weight loss, nutritional deficiency, dependence on supplements/enteral feeding, or psychosocial impairment.
Escalation triggers include growth faltering over 4–8 weeks, extremely narrow food repertoire, or deficiency signs like pallor and recurrent infections.
Management often requires input from medical, dietitian, psychology, and feeding therapy/OT professionals.
reference_quote: ARFID should be considered when restriction causes weight loss or faltering growth, nutritional deficiency, dependence on supplements/enteral feeding, or marked psychosocial impairment. (Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK603710/)
document_key_topics: Vegetarian feeding patterns in selective eating disorders
